s1_reasoning: The kernel consists of simple stencil-style updates over large 2D arrays with very few floating-point operations per element relative to the number of loads/stores. Each iteration streams through ex, ey, and hz repeatedly across time steps. The access pattern is regular and cache-friendly in the inner j dimension, but the arithmetic intensity remains low, so performance is expected to be limited mainly by memory bandwidth rather than compute throughput. There is no inter-process communication in

hotspots.location: kernel_fdtd_2d(), hz update loop: for (i = 0; i < _PB_NX - 1; i++) for (j = 0; j < _PB_NY - 1; j++)
hotspots.reason: This loop updates every interior hz element at every time step and performs multiple dependent loads from ex, ey, and hz with relatively low arithmetic intensity. It has the highest memory traffic per lattice point among the kernel loops.

hotspots.location: kernel_fdtd_2d(), ey update loop: for (i = 1; i < _PB_NX; i++) for (j = 0; j < _PB_NY; j++)
hotspots.reason: This loop sweeps nearly the full ey grid each time step, reading hz[i][j], hz[i-1][j], and ey[i][j], then writing ey[i][j]. The stencil-like access pattern causes substantial memory bandwidth demand.

hotspots.location: kernel_fdtd_2d(), ex update loop: for (i = 0; i < _PB_NX; i++) for (j = 1; j < _PB_NY; j++)
hotspots.reason: This loop also updates nearly the full ex grid each time step with low flop/byte ratio, reading neighboring hz values and ex before writing back. It is similar in cost to the ey loop but slightly lighter depending on cache behavior.

hotspots.location: kernel_fdtd_2d(), boundary loop: for (j = 0; j < _PB_NY; j++) ey[0][j] = _fict_[t]
hotspots.reason: This loop is only one row update per time step, so it is much smaller than the full-grid stencil loops.

bottleneck_type.reasoning: The kernel consists of simple stencil-style updates over large 2D arrays with very few floating-point operations per element relative to the number of loads/stores. Each iteration streams through ex, ey, and hz repeatedly across time steps. The access pattern is regular and cache-friendly in the inner j dimension, but the arithmetic intensity remains low, so performance is expected to be limited mainly by memory bandwidth rather than compute throughput. There is no inter-process communication in this code.

gpu_suitability.reasoning: This is a regular structured-grid stencil computation over dense arrays with predictable memory access and abundant data parallelism across i and j. The three update phases per time step map well to GPU kernels. The main challenge is low arithmetic intensity and the need to preserve phase ordering across time steps, but GPUs can still perform well due to high memory bandwidth.
gpu_suitability.recommended_approach: Port each of the three main update phases to separate GPU kernels per time step, with one additional lightweight kernel for the ey boundary row. Use a 2D thread/block decomposition, keep j as the contiguous dimension for coalesced accesses, and consider shared-memory tiling for hz/ex/ey neighborhoods if it improves reuse. For better performance, explore kernel fusion within tiles or temporal blocking on GPU, but start with straightforward phase-by-phase kernels and profile memory throughput.
